addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

5	// (Full design notes live above createDroneBayView, further down this file.)
6	//
7	// You stand at the ship's command pass. Six subagents rebuild the ship in
8	// parallel; each finished job rides up the pass as silent frozen work:
9	//
10	//   tap silent frozen work → `entire checkpoint explain <id>` runs, the card opens
11	//   read what it did       → DEDUCE its bay and drag the still-sealed block there
12	//   all work installed    → type `entire dispatch` — the day's report writes itself
13	//
14	// The LAUNCH WINDOW (clock) runs the whole time. The real pressure, though, is
15	// Diner Dash / Overcooked PATIENCE: every block on the belt is a waiting customer
16	// whose ICE is its patience meter. Take too long and the ice melts — that work is
17	// LOST and its red dot returns to the dispatch board, costing a whole re-dispatch.
18	
19	// Two pressures: (1) aged dispatch pips on the board heat white -> red and speed the
20	// clock if you leave jobs un-dispatched; (2) each block on the belt burns patience
21	// and SPOILS if you don't explain + install it before its ice melts.

341	// improvised (a star dome, a plasma ring) so you wonder "what did it DO?".
342	//
343	//   tap frozen work     → `entire checkpoint explain <id>` runs, the card opens
344	//   read what it did    → drag the work to the matching ship slot
345	//   all work installed  → type `entire dispatch` right there — the day's report
346	//                          writes itself. Finish line.
347	//
348	// THE CLOCK IS THE LAUNCH WINDOW and it runs the WHOLE time — clearing the pass
349	// with `explain` IS the timed game (the old build made review calm; this one
350	// makes it the rush). Records never expire — a checkpoint is permanent. The
351	// pressure is the flood: subagents finish faster than you can glance, the pass
352	// floods, and the bigger your unreviewed pile the FASTER the launch window
353	// drains (the ship can't stabilise on work nobody has accounted for).
354	// ====================================================================

356	// ---------- canvas-texture text label ----------
357	function makeLabelSprite(text, { color = "#dff1ff", weight = 700, px = 52 } = {}) {
358	  const pad = 24;
359	  const c = document.createElement("canvas");
360	  const ctx = c.getContext("2d");
361	  ctx.font = `${weight} ${px}px "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif`;
362	  const w = Math.ceil(ctx.measureText(text).width) + pad * 2;
363	  const h = px + pad * 2;
364	  c.width = w; c.height = h;
365	  ctx.font = `${weight} ${px}px "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif`;
366	  ctx.textAlign = "center";
367	  ctx.textBaseline = "middle";
368	  ctx.shadowColor = "rgba(0,0,0,0.85)";
369	  ctx.shadowBlur = 10;
370	  ctx.fillStyle = color;
371	  ctx.fillText(text, w / 2, h / 2);
372	  const tex = new THREE.CanvasTexture(c);
373	  tex.anisotropy = 4;
374	  const spr = new THREE.Sprite(new THREE.SpriteMaterial({ map: tex, transparent: true, depthWrite: false }));
375	  spr.scale.set(w / h * 1.4, 1.4, 1);
376	  return spr;
377	}

379	// ---------- the scrolling pass belt texture (chevrons) ----------
380	function makeBeltTexture() {
381	  const c = document.createElement("canvas");
382	  c.width = 128; c.height = 64;
383	  const ctx = c.getContext("2d");
384	  ctx.fillStyle = "#10151f";
385	  ctx.fillRect(0, 0, 128, 64);
386	  ctx.strokeStyle = "rgba(111,227,255,0.5)";
387	  ctx.lineWidth = 9;
388	  for (let x = -64; x < 128; x += 48) {
389	    ctx.beginPath();
390	    ctx.moveTo(x, 8); ctx.lineTo(x + 24, 32); ctx.lineTo(x, 56);
391	    ctx.stroke();
392	  }
393	  const tex = new THREE.CanvasTexture(c);
394	  tex.wrapS = tex.wrapT = THREE.RepeatWrapping;
395	  tex.repeat.set(8, 1);
396	  return tex;
397	}

624	export function createDroneBayView(renderer, { onExit, onComplete, onNext, onNewGame } = {}) {
625	  const canvas = renderer.domElement;
626	
627	  const scene = new THREE.Scene();
628	  scene.background = SKY_CALM.clone();
629	  scene.fog = new THREE.Fog(SKY_CALM.clone(), 90, 260);
630	
631	  const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(58, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 2000);
632	  camera.position.set(0, 10.5, 28);
633	  camera.lookAt(0, 5.5, -6);
634	
635	  scene.add(new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xcdbcff, 0x3a2f5e, 0.85));
636	  const sun = new THREE.DirectionalLight(SUN_CALM, 1.4);
637	  sun.position.set(40, 80, 50); scene.add(sun);
638	  scene.add(new THREE.AmbientLight(0x6a5a92, 0.35));
639	  const dome = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.SphereGeometry(900, 32, 16), new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ color: DOME_CALM.clone(), side: THREE.BackSide, fog: false }));
640	  scene.add(dome);
641	  const floor = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.PlaneGeometry(240, 240), new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x171420, metalness: 0.4, roughness: 0.85 }));
642	  floor.rotation.x = -Math.PI / 2; scene.add(floor);
643	
644	  const beamTex = makeBeamTexture();
645	  const BUILD = {
646	    buildBrokenCoils, buildBrokenNav, buildBrokenDish, buildBrokenVent, buildBrokenStrut,
647	    buildPlasmaRing, buildStarDome, buildGardenPod, buildGravSkid,
648	    buildSignalSpire: () => buildSignalSpire(beamTex),
649	  };
650	
651	  // ---------- conveyor belt ----------
652	  const beltTex = makeBeltTexture();
653	  const belt = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.BoxGeometry(54, 0.9, 6.4), new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ map: beltTex, color: 0x8fb6d6, metalness: 0.3, roughness: 0.6, emissive: 0x16384a, emissiveIntensity: 0.5 }));
654	  belt.position.set(0, BELT_Y - 1.0, 2); scene.add(belt);
655	  const lip = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.BoxGeometry(54, 0.2, 0.4), new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ color: 0xffd27a, transparent: true, opacity: 0.5 }));
656	  lip.position.set(0, BELT_Y - 0.5, 5.2); scene.add(lip);
657	
658	  // ---------- the six labeled ship slots ----------
659	  // Re-rolled each run: which horizontal lane each familiar bay sits in.
660	  const slotXForLane = (lane) => (lane - (SLOT_COUNT - 1) / 2) * SHIP_DX;
661	  let slotLanes = shuffled(SLOT_COUNT);   // slot i occupies lane slotLanes[i]
662	
663	  const slots = SLOT_DATA.map((data, i) => {
664	    const slot = buildShipSlot(data);
665	    const slotPos = new THREE.Vector3(slotXForLane(slotLanes[i]), SHIP_Y, SHIP_Z);
666	    slot.position.copy(slotPos);
667	    slot.userData.slotIdx = i;
668	    scene.add(slot);
669	    return {
670	      data, idx: i, slot, slotPos,
671	      capacity: PART_DATA.filter((p) => p.slotIdx === i).length,
672	    };
673	  });
674	
675	  // ---- the dispatch BOARD: twelve job sockets, arriving over time ----
676	  // Dots are GENERIC job markers: a white dot means "new work waiting"; if it
677	  // sits, it warms toward red. The dot never tells you which bay the work belongs to.
678	  const DOT_COLS = 6;
679	  const boardW = DOT_COLS * SHIP_DX + 6, boardH = 6.6;
680	  const dispatchBoard = new THREE.Mesh(
681	    new THREE.BoxGeometry(boardW, boardH, 0.5),
682	    new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x10141d, emissive: 0x16080a, emissiveIntensity: 0.35, metalness: 0.5, roughness: 0.6 })
683	  );
684	  dispatchBoard.position.set(0, DOT_Y, SHIP_Z - 0.5); scene.add(dispatchBoard);
685	  const boardEdge = new THREE.Mesh(
686	    new THREE.BoxGeometry(boardW + 0.4, boardH + 0.4, 0.3),
687	    new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ color: 0xff5a3c, transparent: true, opacity: 0.32 })
688	  );
689	  boardEdge.position.set(0, DOT_Y, SHIP_Z - 0.66); scene.add(boardEdge);
690	  const dispatchLabel = makeLabelSprite("⚠ DISPATCH", { px: 30, color: "#ff8a5c" });
691	  dispatchLabel.position.set(0, DOT_Y + boardH / 2 - 0.55, SHIP_Z - 0.2); scene.add(dispatchLabel);
692	  const dotXForCol = (col) => (col - (DOT_COLS - 1) / 2) * SHIP_DX;
693	  const dotPosFor = (i) => {
694	    const col = i % DOT_COLS;
695	    const row = Math.floor(i / DOT_COLS);
696	    return new THREE.Vector3(dotXForCol(col), DOT_Y + (row === 0 ? 0.25 : -1.35), SHIP_Z - 0.18);
697	  };
698	
699	  // the little pips — future sockets stay dark; active jobs arrive white
700	  const jobDots = [];
701	  for (let i = 0; i < TOTAL_JOBS; i++) {
702	    const socket = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.CircleGeometry(0.5, 18), new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ color: 0x2a1014 }));
703	    socket.position.copy(dotPosFor(i).clone().setZ(SHIP_Z - 0.22)); scene.add(socket);
704	    const pos = dotPosFor(i);
705	    const group = buildDispatchDot();
706	    group.position.copy(pos); group.userData.dotIdx = i;
707	    group.visible = false;
708	    scene.add(group);
709	    jobDots.push({ idx: i, group, pos, taken: false, spawned: false, partIdx: null, heat: DOT_START_HEAT, wait: 0 });
710	  }
711	  let pendingQueue = shuffled(TOTAL_JOBS);   // order hidden jobs arrive on the dispatch board
712	  let jobsSpawned = 0, spawnTimer = 0;
713	
714	  const drones = [];
715	  const parts = PART_DATA.map((data, i) => {
716	    return {
717	      data, idx: i, targetSlot: data.slotIdx,
718	      state: "queued",             // queued → broken → working → onbelt → review → placed → online
719	      placedIn: null,              // which square this block was dropped into (only the right one sticks)
720	      slateMesh: null, beltX: ENTRANCE_X, explained: false, fixT: 0, installT: 0,
721	      patience: 0,                 // seconds left before the block spoils on the belt
722	      dotIdx: null,
723	    };
724	  });
725	
726	  // ---------- subagent drones (pool) ----------

aiTitle: Review level 2 game design and gameplay feedback

content.text:
I read the whole Level 2 (`src/droneBayView.js`, 1344 lines) plus the design notes baked into it. Here's my honest take on the gameplay — no changes made.

## Does it make sense? Yes.

The loop is clean and readable, and it maps tightly to the thing you're teaching:

- **dispatch** a white pip → a subagent flies out (`dispatchFromDot`, line 919)
- work rides back **sealed in ice** → click to run `entire checkpoint explain` (`explainPart`, 985)
- **read what it did → deduce the bay → drag it there** (`placePart`, 1004)
- all 12 placed → type `entire dispatch` → "look how much got done without you" (1052)

That last beat is the strongest part. The whole level is a thesis — *you didn't do the work, you reviewed and accepted it* — and the ending lands it. One new command (`explain`), consistent with your one-command-per-level rule. Conceptually it's solid.

## Is it fun? Mostly — but I see three real risks.

**1. The "deduction" is too easy — explain risks feeling like a formality.**
The whole point of clicking explain is to *figure out* which bay the work belongs to. But the `did` text gives the answer away verbatim:
- *"engine was seized solid"* → Engine
- *"air scrubbers were dead"* → Air
- *"battery was fried"* → Battery (lines 523–534)

So it's keyword-spotting, not deduction. The interesting field is `became` (Plasma Ring, Garden Pod, Mist Lung) — abstract, makes you think. If you leaned on `became`/`symptom` and made `did` less of a direct label, the read step would actually be a puzzle. Right now a player learns in 30 seconds that they can ignore everything but the first three words.

**2. The two pressures aren't balanced — belt patience does all the work.**
- Belt spoilage is brutal and real: 18s to click + read + drag before ice melts (`PATIENCE`, line 37).
- The launch clock is barely threatened: 150s, and aged pips only add `1 + 0.045 × heat` drain (`DOT_DRAIN`, line 24). Even 3 fully-hot pips ≈ 13% faster. The clock is mostly cosmetic; you'll almost always fail by spoilage, not by time.

So the "launch window" framing oversells a mechanic that rarely bites. The actual game is "don't let blocks rot on the belt." That's fine — but it means your headline pressure (the clock) and your Overcooked framing are carried by one subsystem.

**3. Dispatch decisions are blind, so the early loop is shallow.**
A pip never tells you which bay it is (by design, 677–678). So "which job you dispatch first matters" (the comment at line 496) isn't true from the player's seat — every pip is interchangeable, and the only real choice is "clear the hottest one." With 6 drones and jobs arriving every ~7–11s, drones are rarely all busy, so even that choice is low-stakes. All the actual gameplay depth lives in the belt phase. The dispatch board is mostly a spawner with a heat animation.

## Net

It's a competent, well-polished juggling game that *teaches the right thing* and ends on a genuinely good note. Whether it's *fun* hinges almost entirely on belt congestion — if blocks arrive spread out, the early game feels idle; the fun only spikes when several finish at once. I'd playtest specifically for: does the belt ever actually flood? If not, it's a calm errand, not a rush.

The single highest-leverage change for fun would be making the explain step a real read (lean on `became`, soften `did`) so the core verb earns its place instead of being a click-through.

Want me to dig into the spawn/patience math and sketch some tuning, or look at whether the belt actually floods given the current numbers?

message.content: what do you think of the sections that say 1/2 or 2/2 ? confusing or no?

content.text:
The `1/2` / `2/2` bits are the per-bay capacity hints — the small number under each bay label (`🔧 Engine`, etc.), set at `droneBayView.js:1233`:

```js
const hint = `${occ.length}/${sl.capacity}`;
```

Each bay holds 2 because there are 12 jobs across 6 bays (`capacity` is computed at line 671 from how many PART_DATA rows route to that bay). So a bay reads `0/2` → `1/2` → `2/2` as you fill it.

## My honest take: yes, it's a bit confusing — for two reasons.

**1. It collides with the other `X/Y` readouts on screen.** Right now a player sees several fraction displays at once:
- `0/12` "work installed" (line 878)
- `subagents 4/6`, `jobs 3/12` (lines 880–881)
- and then `1/2` floating under each bay

Four different denominators (2, 6, 12, 12) in the same glance. The `/2` under a bay reads like "1 of 2 *something*" but the player has no reason to know each bay takes exactly two jobs — nothing earlier sets that expectation. So it lands as a number without a referent.

**2. It only makes sense *after* you've already learned the bay takes two.** The first time you install an Engine block and the bay says `1/2` instead of going green/done, that's a small "wait, why isn't this finished?" beat. The number is explaining a rule (bays need two fills) at the moment it surprises you, rather than before. It's doing double duty as both a progress meter and a rule-reveal.

## When it's actually fine

It's genuinely useful as a *"this bay still has room"* signal during the drag — and the code already color-codes that (amber while partial, green `7cffb0` when full and filed, lines 1230–1231). The color is doing the real communicating; the raw `1/2` text is the redundant, confusing part.

## What I'd consider (not changing anything now)

- Drop the fraction and let **color + a fill state** carry it — e.g. an empty bay vs. one installed slate visibly sitting in the holder vs. a full green bay. You can already *see* one block in the bay, so `1/2` is telling you what's on screen.
- Or, if you keep a number, make it a count of slots, not a fraction — two small pips that light up — so it never reads as "1 of 2 of the whole level."
- If you want to keep `1/2`, at minimum say it once in onboarding ("each bay takes two fixes") so the denominator isn't a surprise.

So: not *broken*, but it's the weakest label on the screen — it competes with three other fractions and reveals a rule by surprise. The color states already say the useful part. Want me to look at how much the bays actually rely on that text vs. the holder being visibly occupied?

Base directory for this skill: /private/tmp/claude-501/bundled-skills/2.1.179/40ea5be0c1d0b5fb946a127cc31da4ae/run

**Running means launching the actual app and interacting with it** —
not the test suite, not an `import` of an internal function and a
`console.log`. The app as a user (human or programmatic) would meet
it: the CLI at its command, the server at its socket, the GUI at its
window.

## First: does a project skill already cover this?

A project skill that launches this app is the repo's verified path —
its author already cold-started from a Linux container and committed
what worked: the exact `apt-get` line, the env vars, the patches, the
driver. Use it instead of rediscovering.

```bash
d=$PWD; while :; do
  grep -Hm1 '^description:' "$d"/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md 2>/dev/null
  [ -e "$d/.git" ] || [ "$d" = / ] && break
  d=$(dirname "$d")
done
```

- **One describes launching/driving this app** → read that SKILL.md
  and follow it verbatim. Don't paraphrase; don't skip the patches.
- **Mega-repo, several plausible, no clear match** → ask the user
  which unit to run.
- **Stale** (fails on mechanics unrelated to your task) → tell the
  user; offer to refresh it via `/run-skill-generator`.
- **Nothing about running** → fall back to the patterns below.

## Otherwise: match the shape, use the pattern

Pick the row closest to your project. Each example walks through
launch + first interaction; ignore any trailing "write the skill"
section — you're using the recipe, not authoring one.

| Project type | Handle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| CLI tool | direct invocation, exit code, stdin/stdout | [examples/cli.md](examples/cli.md) |
| Web server / API | background launch + `curl` smoke | [examples/server.md](examples/server.md) |
| TUI / interactive terminal | tmux `send-keys` / `capture-pane` | [examples/tui.md](examples/tui.md) |
| Electron / desktop GUI | Playwright `_electron` REPL under xvfb | [examples/electron.md](examples/electron.md) |
| Browser-driven | dev server + `chromium-cli` script | [examples/playwright.md](examples/playwright.md) |
| Library / SDK | import-and-call smoke script at the package boundary | [examples/library.md](examples/library.md) |

If nothing fits, start from the closest match and adapt. For a web
app, [examples/playwright.md](examples/playwright.md) — drive it with
`chromium-cli`, no custom driver needed. For a desktop app,
[examples/electron.md](examples/electron.md) — it has the `_electron`
REPL driver skeleton and the tmux wrapping.

## Drive it, don't just launch it

Launching with no interaction proves the entrypoint resolves. That's
not running the app — it's typechecking with extra steps. Drive it to
a point where a user would see something:

- CLI → type a representative command, check the exit code and output.
- Server → hit the route the diff touches with `curl`, read the body.
- TUI → `send-keys` a navigation, `capture-pane` the result.
- GUI → click the button, screenshot the window. **Look at the
  screenshot.** A blank frame is a failure to launch.

If the fallback pattern didn't work out of the box — you had to
install packages, set env vars, patch config, or write a driver —
recommend `/run-skill-generator` in your report so that work gets
captured as a project skill. If it just worked, don't.

content.text:
Done with the screenshot — and I left the change itself in place: **12 squares (two per bay), two rows of six, no more `X/2` fraction** (each square just reads empty / amber / green). `npm run build` passes clean. I removed the now-dead `setSlotHint` helper and `SLOT_COUNT` const while I was in there.

Now, the subagents. Here's where they are today:

In `buildDrone`/the pool setup, idle drones park at `(-15 + i*6, 0.5, 6)` — a **row of six in the foreground at `z=6`, while the belt sits at `z=2`**. So from the camera they hover *in front of / on top of* the belt lane where the blocks ride. That's the overlap you're seeing. (They only leave it to fly up to a dispatch pip, weld, and come back.)

One useful fact for deciding: the drone and the block are already **decoupled** — when a subagent finishes, the block just appears at the belt entrance on its own; the drone flies back to its parking spot independently. So the parking spot is purely cosmetic. We can put it anywhere without touching the fix→belt logic.

Where to move them is your call — here are the three that make spatial sense:

questions.question: Where should the idle subagents live (off the belt)?

options.label: Side docks (flanking)
options.description: Two clusters of three parked off each side, beyond the belt ends. Belt lane and foreground stay clear; drones launch inward toward the ship. Cleanest separation.

options.label: Back rail at the ship
options.description: Parked up near the dispatch board where they actually do the repairs. Narratively tightest (the crew lives at the ship), foreground fully clear, but busier up top.

options.label: Lower hangar deck
options.description: A row below the belt; idle drones sit beneath and rise up when dispatched. Keeps the belt lane clear, adds a sense of depth/floor.
